schools.summary: The Sabbath grainfield the loosing of the old law's bonds in the true Sabbath, Christ (Ambrose, Epiphanius, Bede); the bruised ears read mystically of mortifying the old man.
The 'second-first sabbath' (deuteroproton) obscure — variously the feast after unleavened bread or the reckoning toward Pentecost (Gill, Lightfoot, Henry); need not detain faith.
Henry: works of necessity lawful on the Sabbath; ceremonial appointments (the showbread) yield to need — much more the traditions of men.

sides.position: The first sabbath after the second day of unleavened bread (reckoning toward Pentecost)
Uncertain — various festal explanations, but not material to faith

themes: the second sabbath after the first

schools.summary: Cyril of Alexandria: the dispute the old covenant (the old skin) unable to hold the new wine; the Pharisees, ignorant of Scripture, sought only to find fault.
The Pharisees charge the disciples (or Jesus — the Gospels differ; likely both, Gill, Wesley) with what was 'not lawful' by their traditional fence around the Sabbath, not the law itself.

Christ defends the disciples by David's example (1 Samuel 21:6) — the showbread lawful only for priests eaten in need; 'Have ye not read so much as this?' rebukes the Pharisees' professed learning (Robertson), exposing their inconsistency in honouring David yet blaming the disciples.

The showbread (lawful only for priests, Leviticus 24:9) eaten by David in need — one of five defences showing ceremonial law yields to necessity (Robertson); the Codex Bezae addition (the man working on the Sabbath) a textual curiosity, not the received text (Clarke, Robertson).

themes: not lawful but for the priests

schools.summary: Christ Lord of the Sabbath as its ordainer and Legislator (Theophylact); the Sabbath made for man, not man for the Sabbath — its servant, not its master (Chrysostom).
Henry: Christ as Lord of the Sabbath transforms it into the Lord's Day, observed in honour of the Redeemer as it had been of the Creator.

themes: the Son of man is Lord of the sabbath

schools.summary: Christ heals on the Sabbath to convey the spiritual Sabbath (Bede); the withered hand figures humanity unfruitful since the fall, healed by the hand on the cross (Bede); stretch the hand in charity (Ambrose).
Luke alone, with the physician's eye, notes the 'right hand' (Robertson, Ellicott); Clarke rebuts the rationalist who would deny the miracle.
Cyril of Alexandria: the Sabbath law given for mercy — if mercy to the ox, how much more to the diseased man.

The scribes 'watched' (maliciously, insidiously, Clarke, Robertson) for an accusation — a snare in which, healing or not, they hoped to entrap Christ (cruelty if he refused, Sabbath-breaking if he healed).
Bede/Cyril: the envious watching makes the good an occasion for accusation; they lay in wait rather than learn.
Christ's omniscience read their malicious thoughts like an open book (Robertson, Barnes); he commands the man to 'stand forth in the midst,' working openly before all.
Calvin harmonizes: whether they spoke their malice aloud (Matthew) or not, Christ discerned their secret design (Luke).

Christ's counter-question brings the issue into the open (Robertson); to omit good is to do evil (Barnes); 'save or destroy' cuts to the quick — he purposes to save while they plot to destroy him that very day.
Cyril: if it is lawful to do good, cease accusing Christ; if not, you accuse the law itself, given for mercy.

themes: is it lawful to do good

schools.summary: Christ looks round (grieved, Mark adds 'with anger,' Robertson), commands 'Stretch forth thy hand,' and it is restored complete and whole — the man's obedience to the impossible command the means of cure; Clarke rebuts the rationalist.

The Pharisees filled with 'madness' (rage kin to insanity, Robertson) and conspire (with the Herodians, Mark) to destroy Jesus; the miracle that should have convinced only enraged — pride, malice, and disappointed self-confidence combined (Barnes, Clarke).

themes: what they might do to Jesus

schools.summary: Christ prays all night not from weakness but as example (Ambrose, the Author of power); the choice of apostles prefaced by prayer (Gloss); nightly prayer destroys the rust of sin (Chrysostom).
Henry: a whole night in secret prayer before the solemn choice; ministers to be ordained with prayer 'more than ordinarily solemn.'
'In prayer to God' (possibly 'the prayer of God') — Lightfoot notes the rabbinic notion but leaves it undetermined; most take it as Christ's prayer to God.

Christ chose twelve from the larger body of disciples, naming them 'apostles' (those sent); he chose them himself, even Judas (Robertson); they stand apart as personal witnesses to his resurrection (Acts 1:22), without successors in that office.
Calvin: the name 'apostles' given with a view to their future rank, marking their separation and destined office.
The catalogue begins with Simon (named Peter, the rock, for steadfastness, Gill), Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew; the surname Peter given at John 1:42, marked here.
Ambrose: humble fishermen chosen, not the wise or noble, that truth not oratory might prevail; each named (Cyril) to fix the apostolic foundation.

Matthew (the publican/Levi), Thomas (Didymus, the doubter), James of Alphaeus, Simon Zelotes (the zealous, of Cana — not the later murderous party, Clarke); Matthew places himself after Thomas in humility (Bede).
Jude (the brother of James, writer of the epistle) and Judas Iscariot, who 'became' traitor, giving no sign of treachery when chosen (Robertson).

Augustine: Christ chose Judas knowingly, not deceived, willing to be betrayed by his own apostle, that the betrayed might bear it patiently — the false apostle a warning that office is no guarantee of grace.

Christ stands on a 'level place' (Robertson: on the mountain side); the Sermon on the Plain is the same as the Sermon on the Mount — no real difference of place, audience, or topic; Luke's medical interest in 'to be healed.'
Christ descends to the lowly, where the sick are found and healed by his touch (faith, Bede); the Gentiles of Tyre and Sidon present, the apostles appointed publicly for all (Cyril).

debates.question: Is the Sermon on the Plain the same as the Sermon on the Mount?

sides.position: Yes — one sermon; the 'level place' is on the mountain side, no real difference
Possibly distinct, or a repeated/abridged discourse

schools.summary: Those 'vexed' (tormented) by unclean spirits were healed; two verbs for healing used without distinction (Robertson); the cures continual; Christ's authority over the demonic, continued through the apostles (Cyril of Jerusalem).
Healing 'virtue' (power, Luke's medical term, Ellicott) went continually out from Christ and healed all who touched him (Robertson); the power his own as God, not borrowed (Cyril of Alexandria).

Luke's 'poor' (not 'poor in spirit') the actual poor who despise riches (Cyril), yet only the willingly poor joined to Christ's love (Basil); Luke's four blessings contained in Matthew's eight, poverty first as purest virtue (Ambrose).
Henry: the blessing applied directly to the disciples who left all — 'yours is the kingdom of God'; believers may take the gospel promises to themselves.
Christ blesses the poor directly; the great reversal of the gospel — the world's wretched are heirs of the kingdom.

debates.question: Does Luke's 'blessed are ye poor' mean the literally poor or the 'poor in spirit'?

sides.position: The literally poor who despise riches — yet willingly, for Christ's sake
The same as Matthew's 'poor in spirit,' the humble

themes: yours is the kingdom of God

schools.summary: Christ blesses those who hunger and weep 'now' — Luke's adverb sharpening present suffering against future blessing (Robertson); 'laugh' the only NT symbol of spiritual joy (Ellicott).
Both literal and spiritual — hunger after righteousness filled with the joy of virtue (Gregory of Nyssa); godly sorrow the source of pure gladness (Chrysostom, Basil).

themes: ye shall be filled and laugh

schools.summary: The persecution Beatitude: hated, excommunicated ('separate from their company' = synagogue exclusion, John 16:2, Ellicott), name cast out as evil (the contempt on 'Christian,' Clarke, Robertson) — blessed only 'for the Son of man's sake.'
Bede: what men cast out (the name of the Crucified), Christ takes up and raises with himself.

themes: separate you, cast out your name
for the Son of man's sake

schools.summary: Rejoice and 'leap for joy' (Luke's vivid word, also of the unborn John, Ellicott) in persecution, for the reward is great in heaven; the disciples stand in the prophets' persecuted succession (Eusebius).
Chrysostom/Basil: the reward 'great' because promised by the Lord of eternal treasures, and great in itself because given by God.

themes: your reward is great in heaven
as their fathers did to the prophets

schools.summary: Calvin: the woe falls not on all the rich but on those who fix their hope on earth and forget the life to come; 'received your consolation' = drawn the full reward already (Robertson), nothing remaining.
Ambrose: not the money but the heart of the possessor is at fault — those who know not how to use riches are condemned; the Woes balance the Beatitudes (peculiar to Luke).

themes: woe unto you that are rich
not all the rich but the worldly

schools.summary: The full and the laughing shall hunger, mourn, weep — the joys/sorrows of v.21 reversed measure for measure (Robertson), as in Dives and Lazarus (Luke 16); cf. James 4:9 (Ellicott).
Calvin: the woe on the carnally satisfied and those given to Epicurean mirth, lifted up by confidence in present blessings; Basil: no house of laughter for the faithful while multitudes die in sin.

themes: woe to you that are full
woe to you that laugh now

schools.summary: Universal praise (including the wicked's applause) an ill sign — the false prophets were praised, the true persecuted (v.23); truth often lies with the persecuted minority, against 'Vox populi, vox Dei' (Ellicott, Gill).
Calvin: a warning against ambition, especially for teachers, who corrupt doctrine to please men (Galatians 1:10); yet faithful ministers enjoy the applause of good men.

themes: when all men speak well of you
as their fathers did to false prophets

schools.summary: Enemy-love the greatest virtue, the perfection of charity (Ambrose); not merely not to hate but to do good (Chrysostom), regarding persecutors as the physician the mad — fitting for apostles who would call persecutors to salvation (Cyril).
'Which hear' — addressed to all (Henry) or opposed to the Pharisees' traditions (Gill); the command extends love beyond the natural 'hate thine enemy.'

themes: do good to them that hate you

schools.summary: Enemy-love extends to blessing cursers and praying for abusers — the positive returning of good for evil in word and prayer (Gill).
Chrysostom: the mouth given to heal, not bite; praying against enemies defeats one's own prayer — a time for forgiveness, not rage; imitate Christ who prayed for his crucifiers.

themes: pray for them that abuse you

schools.summary: Henry: not rigorous in revenge — offer the other cheek, yield the cloak, leave vengeance to God ('Vengeance is mine'); not forbidding justice but the retaliatory spirit.
Despise property for the soul's sake (Cyril); patient endurance overcomes the wrongdoer's insolence and shames him by meekness (Chrysostom).

themes: forbid not to take thy coat

schools.summary: Henry: free, generous giving to the needy — out of superfluities ordinarily, beyond one's power extraordinarily; ready to distribute and communicate.
Give what is just and honest, sometimes correcting the unworthy seeker (Augustine), yet not using idleness as an excuse for hardness (Chrysostom); all we have is God's, held as stewards.

themes: give to every man that asketh

schools.summary: Henry: the golden rule the summary of love — put your soul in the other's place and do as you would be done by, in justice and charity.
Chrysostom: its positive form (do good, not merely abstain) surpasses the rabbis' negative rule; it appeals to the natural law in the conscience.

themes: as ye would that men should do

schools.summary: Reciprocal love is common to all, even sinners; spiritual love descends from heaven and embraces enemies (Chrysostom — Paul, Moses loving persecutors).

'Sinners' = Luke's wider term (for Gentiles) for Matthew's 'publicans' (Ellicott); reciprocal love deserves no 'thank' or reward (Robertson); Wesley: returning love for love is no mark of Christianity.

themes: if ye love them which love you

schools.summary: Doing good to those who do good is no more than sinners do (the whole class of outcasts, Robertson); Luke makes it concrete in deeds (where Matthew has salutations, Ellicott) — reciprocal good-doing no mark of the kingdom.

themes: if ye do good to them that do good

schools.summary: Lending in hope of return (with or without interest, Robertson) is what sinners do; interest not contemplated among Jewish hearers (Ellicott) — the third instance of reciprocal virtue, no mark of the kingdom.
Tertullian: the law forbade usury as preparatory to the gospel, which goes further to lending without hope of return.

themes: if ye lend to them of whom ye hope

schools.summary: Love enemies, do good, lend 'never despairing' (the better reading — not losing heart at the absence of return, Ellicott, Robertson); the Vulgate's misreading wrongly led to condemning all interest; children of the Highest who imitate God's kindness to the unthankful.
Calvin: not about usury but disinterested liberality — believers exceed heathens, lending freely; mercenary acts of no account before God; the great reward is God himself.
Clarke: 'the most sublime precept ever delivered to man,' impracticable without supernatural grace.

debates.question: Does 'hoping for nothing again' mean expecting no repayment, or 'never despairing'?

sides.position: 'Never despairing' — not losing heart at the absence of return (the better Greek reading)
Expecting no repayment — disinterested liberality, not a rule about usury

themes: love your enemies, do good, lend

schools.summary: 'Be merciful as your Father is merciful' — Luke's form of Matthew's 'be perfect'; mercy the noblest divine attribute, in which all others are completed (Ellicott); the goal set before the children, not yet attained (Robertson).
Clarke: the merciful man, like God, helps the miserable, forgets injuries, pardons unsolicited, and does good to the unthankful.

'Judge not' forbids the censorious spirit (judging while guilty, judging superiors and the innocent, Ambrose, Chrysostom), not judgment with pardon (Gregory of Nyssa) or loving correction; forgive, and be forgiven (Bede).
The threefold escalation (judge, condemn, forgive) with reciprocal promise — as we deal, so shall we be dealt with; flowing from the mercy of v.36.

Generous giving met with overflowing recompense — good measure pressed down, running over, into the bosom (the garment's fold, Robertson); rabbinic background on Temple measures (Lightfoot); God repays charity with 'excessive interest' (Clarke).
Wesley: 'Amazing goodness' — we determine our own recompense by our generosity; give to man what you would receive of God.

themes: give and it shall be given

schools.summary: The blind guide warns teachers (who must be illuminated, Cyril) and the censorious (who judge while sinning, Theophylact, Bede) — both blind and blind will fall.
A 'parable' in the wider sense of proverb, opening the Sermon's latter half (Robertson); connects to the mote-and-beam — self-correction must precede guiding others.

themes: can the blind lead the blind

schools.summary: The disciple becomes like his master when 'perfected' (thoroughly instructed, formed into resemblance, Clarke, Robertson); the blind guide produces blind followers (Barnes); the disciple of Christ models himself on the Master who did not judge (Ellicott).
Calvin: the sense is resemblance, not perfection — the disciple formed after his master's example.

themes: the disciple is not above his master

schools.summary: The mote (speck) and beam (rafter) expose the absurdity of censorious hypocrisy — scrutinizing the brother's small fault while blind to one's own great sin; the disproportion deliberately exaggerated (Robertson).
Theophylact: applies especially to teachers who punish small sins in others while leaving their own unpunished.

themes: the mote in thy brother's eye

schools.summary: The hypocrite offers to remove the brother's mote while blind to his own beam — feigned 'Brother' against rebuking 'Thou hypocrite' (Robertson); the remedy is to cast out one's own beam first, then help the brother.
Theophylact: the hypocrisy is judging others to seem just oneself; the fathers: first be cleansed of great sins, then counsel a brother's slight ones.

themes: let me pull out the mote

schools.summary: Character tested by conduct — the hypocrite's evil works belie his fair words (Bede); but the evil tree may yet be converted by repentance (Isidore), and man has free will, unlike the tree (Titus Bostrensis).
Calvin/Gill: by the fruit our opinion of every man (and teacher) is to be formed; the heart's character determines the conduct.

The tree known by its fruit — thorns yield no figs, brambles no grapes; so a corrupt heart yields no good works, false doctrine no good fruit (Gill); Luke's form differs slightly from Matthew's (Ellicott).

themes: every tree known by its fruit

schools.summary: The heart the treasure/root from which conduct and speech flow (Bede); wicked words reveal an even more copious fountain of wickedness within (Chrysostom).

The mouth speaks from the heart's abundance — speech the index of the mind (Calvin), the stream revealing the fountain, though hypocrites may mask their hearts (Robertson); good treasure brings forth compassion, evil bitterness (Ellicott).

themes: the good treasure of the heart
of the abundance of the heart

schools.summary: Christ rebukes the leaves of confession without the fruit of works (Bede); to call him 'Lord' confesses his deity (Athanasius), but the confession is empty without obedience.
Clarke/Wesley: God judges by works, not words; fair professions avail nothing without a life to match — the tongue empties but neither fills nor cleanses the heart.

themes: why call ye me Lord, Lord
and do not what I say

schools.summary: The true disciple marked by three joined acts — coming (faith), hearing (instruction), doing (obedience, present participles of continual practice, Robertson); Christ promises a plain illustration (the parable).

The rock is Christ (Cyril) / the faith of Christ (Basil) / obedience to his precepts (Ambrose); the house so founded is unshaken by the floods of pleasure, spiritual wickedness, worldly storms, and heresy.
'Digged and went deep' Luke's distinctive detail — the wise builder struck the rock first (Robertson); the house not even shaken 'because it had been well built' (Ellicott).

The mere hearer is the foolish builder 'without a foundation' (Luke's specific phrase, Ellicott) on the earth, whose house falls at once in the flood — great is the ruin (Clarke: all ruinous without a holy heart in the day of wrath).
Bede: sin has no foundation; the flood is the last judgment, in which the foundationless house collapses; faith profits nothing if the life is corrupt (Chrysostom).

themes: the ruin of the mere hearer
